briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for April 9, 2026. Today, we primarily covered the severity of the global climate crisis, along with various policy discussions taking place domestically and internationally, examples of carbon neutrality practices, and efforts towards renewable energy transition.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Crisis Warnings and Direct Impacts at Home and Abroad
sections.content: Key reports released today once again emphasized the severity of the climate crisis, noting that ten years after the Paris Agreement, the 1.5℃ global temperature rise defense line is on the verge of collapse, with its progress report rated 'red' [12]. Specifically, the continental United States experienced record-breaking heatwaves as March temperatures surpassed previous highs [107, 111, 117], and India is also facing an imminent heatwave crisis, planning how to confront climate change [120]. Domestically, climate change has led to concerns about changes in wine taste as wine regions and cultivation methods are being disrupted [3], and agricultural sectors are directly hit by the climate crisis, with Chungnam Liriope farmers suffering direct damage [7]. Furthermore, concerns were raised about Korea's preparedness due to the suggested link between climate change and emerging infectious diseases [13].

sections.title: 🌍 International and Domestic Landscape of Climate Policy and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Internationally, India withdrew from the race to host the 2028 UN Climate Change Conference (COP), raising questions about the future direction of international climate cooperation [2, 11]. Domestically, Gyeongsangnam-do held a meeting to address fishing grounds affected by climate change [1], and Muju-gun invested 260 million won in disaster prevention to prepare for the climate crisis, strengthening its 'proactive agricultural policy' [10]. At the 2026 Seoul Climate Conference, a climate crisis action platform connecting policy, technology, and finance was presented [5]. The Korea Environment Institute and Anyang City are exploring policy responses by conducting climate crisis response policy symposiums and implementation reviews [6, 14, 27]. Meanwhile, in the United States, contrasting policy stances are emerging, with the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator praising groups opposing government action on climate change and advocating for the repeal of climate change policies [103, 110].

sections.title: 🌱 Concrete Actions by Local Governments and Businesses Towards Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: In today's briefing, the commitment of businesses to carbon neutrality was prominent, with Korea Southern Power actively responding to the climate crisis by creating a 'Carbon Neutral Forest' in Hadong to commemorate its 25th anniversary [8, 16, 25, 26, 32, 36, 38, 40, 41, 44, 48, 50, 54, 59, 61, 62, 65, 67, 73]. Local governments have also embarked on concrete actions to achieve carbon neutrality goals. Goyang City shifted its carbon neutrality policy direction from an 'era of declarations' to 'implementation and performance management,' initiating full-scale implementation reviews [17, 18, 19, 24, 31, 55, 58, 66, 71]. Anyang City exceeded its greenhouse gas reduction targets through 55 projects, fully launching its carbon-neutral operations [20, 72]. HD Hyundai took a significant step towards carbon neutrality in the shipping sector by building the world's first ammonia-powered vessel [33], and Gyeongbuk Province is promoting customized, action-oriented consulting to support small and medium-sized enterprises in their transition to carbon neutrality [39, 56].

sections.title: 🌱 Spreading Carbon Neutrality Through Everyday Practices and Education
sections.content: Carbon neutrality is now spreading throughout civil society through everyday practices and education. Syngenta Korea explored the future direction of agriculture by presenting regenerative agriculture and climate response solutions with young farmers [4]. The Yongin Special City Carbon Neutrality Support Center is expanding its 'Visiting Carbon Neutrality Citizen Education' to senior welfare centers [28], and students at various schools, including Gimpo Geumpa Elementary, Samsan Elementary, and Noannam Elementary, are directly practicing carbon neutrality through activities like plogging and tree planting [15, 43, 69]. Furthermore, diverse entities are making efforts to spread the value of carbon neutrality in daily life, such as practicing carbon neutrality using discarded coconut shells [23], Paju Saemaul Association's used clothing collection event [34, 70], and Gwangju Shinsegae's 'Earth Day' carbon neutrality campaign [30, 76]. These efforts are driving voluntary citizen participation and becoming a crucial force accelerating the transition to a carbon-neutral society.

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Facing Challenges
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is a global trend, actively pursued both domestically and internationally. The UK is accelerating its renewable energy transition by approving the construction of its largest-ever 800MW solar farm [79], and Hanwha is strongly reaffirming its commitment to fostering new and renewable energy, expanding related investments [77, 95]. Jeonbuk Self-Governing Province is fully launching its RE100 implementation and promoting renewable energy policies [78], while the Chungnam Provincial Council is initiating a 'renewable energy model' that involves resident participation and profit-sharing, laying the institutional groundwork [88, 90, 97]. However, challenges faced during the expansion of renewable energy have also become clear. In China, solar power curtailment soared to 9.2%, putting a 'brake' on renewable energy expansion [82], and Europe is also facing situations where renewable energy expansion efforts are delayed or halted due to grid bottlenecks [98]. These challenges once again highlight the importance of expanding renewable energy infrastructure and stabilizing the grid.

briefing.closing: As you have seen in today's briefing, the climate crisis is sending urgent warnings across all aspects of our lives. At the same time, the proactive efforts of governments, businesses, and civil society worldwide towards carbon neutrality and renewable energy transition are becoming crucial steps for a hopeful future. Moving forward, we must continue to build a sustainable Earth through in-depth discussions and concrete actions on climate change. This concludes today's climate briefing.
